nativeRange.habitat: Shallow reef and intertidal zones with moderate to high water movement

decorPreferences: open placement or loose attachment to rockwork

avoidWith: aggressive herbivorous fish that may graze heavily on macroalgae

summary: Bluish-Purple Ochtodes (Ochtodes sp.) is a rare ornamental red alga with cylindrical branching fronds that display an iridescent bluish-purple sheen under full-spectrum reef lighting. It is a specialty species available from a small number of specialist macroalgae retailers.
careNotes:
Bluish-Purple Ochtodes is a red alga in the family Rhizophyllidaceae with a cylindrical, dichotomously branching thallus — a bushy structure of repeatedly forking, tube-like branches. The iridescent bluish-purple color is a structural coloration effect rather than pigment, meaning it depends on light angle and spectrum. Specimens tend to arrive looking grey or muted and generally color up over several days once placed under adequate full-spectrum lighting. This initial grey appearance is normal and does not indicate a health problem.

Care parameters are drawn from genus- and family-level literature for Gigartinales red macroalgae, the order to which Ochtodes belongs. Standard reef water conditions are generally advised: salinity SG 1.023–1.026, temperature 23–28°C, and pH 8.1–8.4. Medium, moderate full-spectrum lighting is generally recommended; excessive light intensity has been reported to cause bleaching or browning in related species. Moderate to high flow helps deliver nutrients and prevents debris from settling on the thallus. No supplemental fertilization is typically required in an established reef system, though trace dosing of iron and iodine may benefit growth.

Ochtodes species have been documented producing halogenated monoterpenes (myrcene and related compounds) with mild antimicrobial properties. No allelopathic effects on corals — that is, no chemical suppression of neighboring coral growth — have been reported at typical display densities, and the genus is generally considered reef-safe.

Important for attachment: as a red alga, this species will MELT at the base if buried in sand or substrate. It should be attached to rockwork using superglue, zip-tie, fishing line, or wedging rather than planted into the sand bed.

Note: some online listings for this species include the genus name Hypnea in their URLs or titles due to earlier trade confusion. Ochtodes is a distinct genus from Hypnea, with noticeably thicker, more cylindrical branches and a deeper purple hue than Hypnea pannosa.
breedingNotes: Aquarium propagation is typically by fragmentation: detaching a portion of the thallus and anchoring it to rockwork or rubble where it can reattach and grow. Sexual reproduction via alternation of gametophyte and sporophyte generations occurs in wild populations but has not been documented as a practical propagation route in the hobby.

primary.name: Mosaic Macros
primary.notes: Only known hobby trade source for this specialty species. Confirms scientific name Ochtodes sp., iridescent bluish-purple coloration, cylindrical branching form, and grey-on-arrival coloration behavior. No numeric water parameters provided by source.

additional.name: AlgaeBarn — Blue Hypnea (Hypnea pannosa) care page
additional.notes: Used as secondary reference for Gigartinales red macroalgae water parameters (temperature 73–83°F / 23–28°C, SG 1.023–1.025, pH 7.8–8.4). Ochtodes and Hypnea both belong to order Gigartinales, supporting parameter extrapolation. Confirmed medium lighting and fragmentation propagation for this group.

additional.name: Mosaic Macros general macroalgae care guide
additional.notes: Slice-wide secondary source — provides authoritative Mosaic-wide parameter targets (temp 22-26°C, pH 8.1-8.4, Ca 400-450 ppm, Mg 1250-1350 ppm, NO3 5-20 ppm, PO4 0.02-0.1 ppm) and the 4-method propagation standard. Added retroactively 2026-06-08.
